text: Hello and welcome to This is my architecture. Today with me I have Vasilisa from MyTona.
Hi. So Vasilisa can you tell us a little bit about MyTona? Sure. MyTona is a game developer
and publisher company. We create mobile games in the time management and hidden object genres.
Okay, so mobile games is it. So what's this interesting architecture that you have for us today?
Yeah, for our games we were solving the use case of more personalized and relevant offers. So me and
my machine learning team we built an in-game purchase recommendation system which is called
a Mal-Offer. Okay, so the system with its own name that's very interesting. So what does it actually do?
Yeah, our system has two parts. The one that makes predictions and the other is responsible for
models training. So when game servers request predictions, the Lambda function gets triggered
by API invokment and takes on the role of a reverse proxy. It allocates traffic between our recommender
models which are placed in the ECS cluster. We also have one container for Bandit here, multi-arm Bandit.
Okay, so that's a very interesting piece. I suggest it allocates the traffic dynamically,
but how does it actually work? Yeah, in our Lambda function we implemented dynamic traffic allocation.
In the multi-arm Bandit collection, the multi-arm Bandit collects data from Kafka on the way users reacted
for different models predictions and periodically recounts weights.
Okay, so it assigns the different weights to the models, I guess? Yeah.
Yeah, so basically we have our inner continuous A-B testing between our models.
Okay, so there's an additional use case which is A-B testing, very interesting. Yeah.
But for machine learning there is additional interesting thing which is a model training.
How do you actually train your models? Sure. For model training we use SageMaker which gets
daily triggered by a managed workflow in EC2-based Airflow. Which is a workflow manager, I suggest.
Yeah, it collects data from Kafka and eventually our model is stored in S3 buckets. From where they are
gathered to build models API containers. And we also use ECR to store our API images for different models.
Okay, so that's very interesting. But definitely while building the system you might have had some
challenges and problems. What were those? Yeah, you see the ECS cluster and Lambda were
not our first choices. At the first we implemented manually Docker Swarm on EC2 instances with TrafficFord
traffic allocation. And it brought a lot of problems with scaling and adding new versions of models.
Yeah, okay. So what were these issues? You see the requests are not evenly distributed within a day.
So we had to manually add new replicas, create new instances or change their capacity to handle traffic spikes.
Okay, kind of a challenge. How did you actually solve it?
Yeah, actually switching to this version of architecture were our solution. Having our models as ECS containers allows us to create triggers for auto-scaling and to safely add new versions of models.
So problem solved is it? Yeah. Okay, so you've been running this system for some time already. So what were actually the performance and the outcomes of the system?
Yeah, according to test results, our system performed much better than the one without machine learning personalization.
We increased our conversion rates three times and our revenue was boosted four times for this type of offers.
Okay, but how actually many customers is this system used per day?
Yeah, it's like several hundreds of thousands.
Okay, that's impressive. Well, thank you Vasilisa for sharing this information with us today.
Thank you for having me. And thank you for watching This Is My Architecture.
